coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven photothermal water evaporation offers a promising alternative for fresh water production, necessitating efficient and sustainable photothermal materials. Spent coffee grounds, an abundant biowaste, are a potential biomass-based evaporation material for this application, though limited by relatively low evaporation efficiency and the need for additional materials. Here, we present a hydrothermal-treated spent coffee grounds (DA-CGs) that contains carbon dots with strong photothermal conversion abilities, serving as an efficient solar evaporation material. Additionally, this material can also reduce water evaporation enthalpy by promoting intermediate water formation, while their hierarchical structures enhance sunlight trapping and expands the liquid-vapor interface. These features collectively contribute to their high efficiency in solar-driven water evaporation. The DA-CGs evaporator demonstrated a high evaporation rate of 2.45 kg m−2 h−1 for pure water under 1 sun and an outdoor daily water yield of 7.14 kg m−2 when purifying seawater. These advantages highlight its potential for large-scale water purification and provide valuable insights into utilizing biowaste for efficient solar water evaporation.

Characterizations of DA-CGs. (a) PL emission and excitation curve of C-CDs. (b) TEM image of C-CDs. Inset is the high-resolution TEM image (bottom-left) and size distribution histogram (top-right). (c) XRD patterns of DA-CGs and C-CDs. (d) Raman spectra of C-CDs and DA-CGs. (e) ATR-FTIR spectra of CGs, DA-CGs, C-CDs and DA. (f) XPS element survey spectrum and deconvoluted XPS C1s (g), N1s (h), O1s (i) spectra of C-CDs.

Solar-driven water evaporation performance. (a) Solar irradiance spectra (AM1.5 Global ASTMG173) and UV–Vis-NIR diffuse light reflectance spectra of DA-CGs in dry and wet states. (b) IR thermal images and (c) measured temperatures of the surface of dried DA-CGs, C-CDs and CGs under 1 sun irradiance. (d) Illustration of DA-CGs evaporation and experimental setup for the indoor simulated solar-driven water evaporation tests. (e) IR thermal images and (f) measured temperatures of the surface of DA-CGs pile on evaporators with pure water or saline water. (g) Mass change and (h) evaporation rate of DA-CGs evaporator for pure water or saline water under 1 sun irradiance. (i) Long-term evaporation test of DA-CGs in 10 wt% sodium chloride solution under 1 sun irradiance.

(a) Evaporation enthalpies of water in DA-CGs measured by the DSC isothermal (45 °C) method. Evaporation enthalpies of pure water and sodium chloride solution are given by literature [54]. (b) Ratio of intermediate water to free water (IF/IW) calculated from the Raman spectra. (c) Evaporation rate under dark condition of DA-CGs evaporator compared to pure water or sodium chloride solutions with the same apparent evaporation surface area. SEM images of the granule surfaces of (d) CGs and (e) DA-CGs at the micro-nano scale, the micropores on granules of (f) CGs and (g) DA-CGs, and the fixated DA-CGs pile taken at (h) 45° and (i) 90°, highlighting its canyon-like micro-3D surface.

(a) Illustration of the solar desalination device. (b) Digital image of the outdoor testing environment. (c) Average water yield, solar irradiance and air temperature measured at different hours on August 22, 2024. (d) Daily water yield, accumulative solar irradiation and average air temperature measured for 10 h on seven days from August to September 2024.

head.ce:title: Architecting spent coffee grounds for highly efficient solar water evaporation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven photothermal water evaporation offers a promising alternative for fresh water production, necessitating efficient and sustainable photothermal materials. Spent coffee grounds, an abundant biowaste, are a potential biomass-based evaporation material for this application, though limited by relatively low evaporation efficiency and the need for additional materials. Here, we present a hydrothermal-treated spent coffee grounds (DA-CGs) that contains carbon dots with strong photothermal conversion abilities, serving as an efficient solar evaporation material. Additionally, this material can also reduce water evaporation enthalpy by promoting intermediate water formation, while their hierarchical structures enhance sunlight trapping and expands the liquid-vapor interface. These features collectively contribute to their high efficiency in solar-driven water evaporation. The DA-CGs evaporator demonstrated a high evaporation rate of 2.45 kg m−2 h−1 for pure water under 1 sun and an outdoor daily water yield of 7.14 kg m−2 when purifying seawater. These advantages highlight its potential for large-scale water purification and provide valuable insights into utilizing biowaste for efficient solar water evaporation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing global population, climate change and environmental pollution caused by rapid industrialization have exacerbated the shortage of fresh water, making it a pressing global issue that remains unresolved [1]. This scarcity has driven the development of seawater desalination and purification technologies, leading to advancement of mature methods, such as the reverse osmosis (RO) technology based on membrane separation and multiple-effect desalination (MED) utilizing thermal distillation [2]. However, these technologies require substantial consumption of fossil fuels and electricity, not only leaving carbon footprint in the desalination process but also making them unsuitable for regions with energy shortages, underdeveloped infrastructure, or those in remote locations. In contrast, solar-driven photothermal water evaporation has emerged as a promising alternative, offering advantages like sustainability, low cost and environmental friendliness, and have become a significant focus of advanced research.

Increasing the efficiency of solar water evaporation relies on enhancing sunlight trapping, reducing heat loss, promoting liquid-vapor transformation and resisting salt precipitation. To achieve these, research in solar water evaporation focuses extensively on optimizing system designs and developing advanced photothermal materials to achieve higher efficiency [3]. Currently, photothermal materials for solar-driven photothermal water evaporation can be roughly categorized in to four types: metals and metal oxides, such as gold nanoparticles and titanium oxide [4,5]; polymers, including polypyrrole coating and polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel [6,7]; carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes and reduced graphene oxide [8]; and natural biomass, including wood [9,10] and straw [11]. These materials often intersect and are combined to form composite structures that enhance photothermal evaporation performance. Natural biomass is utilized for its low cost, environmental friendliness, ease of fabrication, and inherent structures that facilitate water transportation and evaporation [1]. However, most natural biomass materials exhibit relatively weak solar absorption and photothermal conversion, often necessitating the use of additional photothermal materials or carbonization. Compared to incorporating external photothermal materials, carbonization offers a simple, scalable and cost-effective approach for preparing biomass-based photothermal evaporation materials. However, excessive carbonization can compromise the hydrophilicity and natural structures of the biomass, which not only hinders water transport but also eliminates the potential to reduce evaporation enthalpy [12]. In some cases, this may necessitate the addition of materials such as hydrogels or aerogels to achieve specific structural configurations, similar to those used in typical carbon-based materials [13–16].

Coffee is one of the world's most popular and consumed beverages, with annual production consistently exceeding 10 million tons [17], yet resulting in the generation of nearly 8 million tons of spent coffee grounds [18]. Even worse, the simple disposal of used coffee grounds also releases toxic organic compounds, including polyphenols, caffeine, and tannins, into the soil or water environment [18]. Nonetheless, spent coffee grounds represent an underutilized biowaste with considerable potential for various applications [19]. One promising area of research involves their use as photothermal materials in applications such as solar energy storage [20], laser photothermal conversion [21], and solar water evaporation, though studies in this field are still emerging. Among these, solar water evaporation has garnered particular attention. For example, Ma et al. investigated the solar evaporation performance of unmodified spent coffee grounds and achieved a final interface temperature of 63.2 °C and an evaporation rate of 0.88 kg m−2 h−1 under 0.25 sun irradiation [22]. Wang et al. developed a self-floating photothermal material for solar water evaporation using high-temperature carbonization and hydrophobic silane modification of spent coffee grounds [23]. Dong et al. combined spent coffee grounds with calcium alginate hydrogel, achieving an evaporation rate of 1.59 kg m−2 h−1 [14]. Additionally, Shi et al. reported an evaporation rate of 1.54 kg m−2 h−1 by in-situ growing polypyrrole on delignified spent coffee grounds [18]. However, the utilizations of spent coffee grounds for this purpose typically required high-temperature carbonization, doping of additional photothermal materials, or usage of structural materials such as gels, while the relatively low evaporation efficiency continues to limit the advancement of spent coffee grounds for solar water evaporation. Nonetheless, spent coffee grounds possess granular features and porous structures, both of which can facilitate sunlight trapping and enhance water evaporation. In addition, as a typical lignocellulosic biomass, spent coffee grounds contain approximately 30.3 % lignin and 13.2 % cellulose by weight [18]. The lignin content of spent coffee grounds is relatively higher than typical lignocellulosic biomasses [24–26], and since lignin is more easily carbonized [27], it can impart the light absorption and photothermal conversion properties to the material. The considerable amount of hydrophilic cellulose in the spent coffee grounds also has the potential to promote water transport and reduce water evaporation enthalpy [1,28]. Luo et al. extracted cellulose nanofibers from spent coffee grounds via TEMPO-mediated oxidation and combined them with rGO to fabricate aerogel evaporators, achieving an evaporation rate of 2.73 kg m−2 h−1 [29]. Above all, our goal is to develop a material derived from spent coffee grounds that exhibits outstanding photothermal conversion properties while retaining its natural structures to enhance the photothermal evaporation process without the need for additional photothermal or structural materials.

In specific carbonization processes of biomass raw materials, a carbon-based material known as carbon dots can be generated [30]. Carbon dots are a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size less than 20 nm [31]. A carbon dot typically consists of a sp2/sp3-hybridized carbonaceous core surrounded by surface functional groups. This unique nanostructure endows carbon dots with excellent light absorption and photoluminescent properties. Therefore, they are widely used as photoluminescent materials for applications such as bioimaging, sensors and anti-counterfeiting [30]. Utilizing the photoluminescent features of carbon dots involves achieving a high photoluminescent intensity or quantum yield. However, when the quantum yield is low, the characteristic of strong light absorption can still remain, and the excess absorbed light energy is converted to heat via the non-radiative transition process of photoelectrons [32]. Therefore, carbon dots with strong light absorption can also effectively function as photothermal conversion materials [33,34].

Herein, we utilized spent coffee grounds (CGs) to fabricate a material incorporating photothermal carbon dots for solar-driven water evaporation, via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of the coffee grounds and dopamine. This material, referred to as DA-CGs, features both intrinsic and structural characteristics. The intrinsic characteristics of DA-CGs include: (i) The carbon dots, primarily derived from lignin in the spent coffee grounds and doped with nitrogen from dopamine, exhibit excellent light absorption and photothermal conversion abilities. (ii) Additionally, DA-CGs promote the formation of intermediate water and reduce the water evaporation enthalpy, thereby facilitate the liquid-vapor phase transition process. The structural characteristics of DA-CGs are as follows: (iii) The canyon-like micro-3D surface of the granular DA-CGs, the native micropores formed by cell walls of coffee beans, and the nanospheres formed on the granule surfaces collectively create the hierarchical structures that enhance sunlight trapping and increase the liquid-vapor interfacial area. Overall, DA-CGs in wet state achieve an average absorptance of 94.6 % across the AM1.5 global solar spectrum. The DA-CGs evaporator demonstrates evaporation rates of 2.45 kg m−2 h−1 in pure water and 2.35 kg m−2 h−1 in 10 wt% sodium chloride solution under 1 sun irradiance. Under real-world conditions, a desalination device incorporating the DA-CGs evaporator can achieve a daily water yield of 7.14 kg m−2 with an accumulative solar radiation of 19.93 MJ m−2 when feeding actual seawater. Our results demonstrate that the DA-CGs exhibit superior photothermal conversion ability and evaporation performance as a biomass-derived material, highlighting their potential for scalable water purification applications and providing new insights into the effective utilization of biowaste in solar-driven photothermal water evaporation.

The coffee beans utilized in this study were 100 % Arabica sourced from Lavazza, specifically Lavazza Qualità Oro. The coffee grounds were obtained after grinding and brewing the coffee beans using a coffee maker (HD7761, Philips, Netherlands). Dopamine hydrochloride, lignin, (+)-arabinogalactan (extracted from larch wood), methyl orange, methylene blue, potassium chromate, sodium chloride and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Macklin, China. Copper sulfate pentahydrate was purchased from Aladdin, China. Sulfuric acid (95–98 %) was purchased from Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. Galactomannan (food grade) was purchased from Guangdong Kangda Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Actual seawater was collected from Qingdao, Shandong, China (35°53′N, 120°8′E).

DA-CGs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of coffee grounds and dopamine hydrochloride. In a typical preparation, 0.25 g dopamine was dissolved into 10 mL pure water and mixed uniformly. This dopamine solution was combined with 2.5 g of coffee grounds and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was then heated at 200 °C in an oven for 12 h. Following the reaction, the solid-liquid resultant was dialyzed against pure water using 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane. The dialyzed resultant was thoroughly dried at 80 °C in an oven to obtain DA-CGs.

Carbon dots suspensions were acquired following dialysis of the hydrothermal resultant. The dialyzed resultant was centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant containing carbon dots. Subsequently, the supernatant was purified with 0.22 μm filtration membrane to acquire the filtrate as carbon dots suspensions. This type of pristine carbon dots suspensions was used for photoluminescence characterization and the observation of carbon dots using TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch preparation of dried carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Dried carbon dots were extracted following the hydrothermal treatment. The hydrothermal resultant was further ground using a pair of agate mortar and pestle. The pH of the ground product suspension was adjusted to 7.0 using a pH meter and a 2.0 M sodium hydroxide solution. The neutralized resultant was then centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 10 min to collect the supernatant. The supernatant was subsequently purified with 0.22 μm filtration membrane and then dialyzed using 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane to obtain the carbon dots suspensions. Dried carbon dots were acquired by lyophilizing the carbon dots suspensions for approximately 2 days. This type of dried carbon dots was used for photothermal temperature ramping tests and characterized using Raman spectrometer, XRD spectrometer, FT-IR spectrometer and XPS.
Compressed DA-CGs film was prepared by compressing 0.4 g of dry DA-CGs in a stainless-steel column mold with a diameter of 2 cm under a pressure of 5 tons for 3 min.

Hydrothermal treated dopamine hydrochloride or coffee grounds were denoted as HT-DA and HT-CGs. HT-DA was prepared with only 0.25 g DA and 10 mL water added to the autoclave. HT-CGs were prepared with only 2.5 g CGs and 10 mL water added to the autoclave.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal treatment of lignocellulosic model components
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of dopamine, hydrothermal treated lignin, galactomannan, arabinogalactan or bacterial cellulose were prepared using the same method as the preparation of DA-CGs and denoted as DA-Lignin, DA-GM, DA-AG and DA-Cellulose, respectively. The main components were used in the following quantities, approximately based on the ratio of components of spent coffee grounds: 0.75 g of lignin, 0.5 g of galactomannan, 0.3 g of arabinogalactan, 0.25 g of bacterial cellulose.

As illustrated in Fig. 3d, DA-CGs evaporator is basically consisted of three main components: a 50 mL beaker serving as a water container, two circular white polypropylene foam pieces acting as isothermal layers, and a piece of circular quantitative filter paper (30–50 μm, medium speed) with two feet placed between the two foam layers for water absorption. The beaker was filled with either pure water or sodium chloride solutions of known concentrations. The bottom foam layer floated freely on the liquid surface and was fully covered by the circular filter paper. The top foam layer upon the filter paper, with a thickness of 5 mm, featured a centrally positioned circular through-hole with a radius of 7.5 mm. Approximately 0.2 g of dry DA-CGs, pre-wetted with pure water or sodium chloride solutions accordingly, were piled into the through-hole on the top foam layer and manually leveled to align with the height of the top foam layer using a lab spatula. To reduce the systematic error caused by water evaporating from the tiny gap between the foam layers and the side wall of the beaker, parafilm was applied on the top of the gap according to the method proposed by R. Fillet, et al. [35].

The as-prepared carbon dots suspension was placed in a 10 mm four-way light fluorescence quartz cuvette. The cuvette was then placed in a photoluminescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) to obtain the photoluminescent (PL) emission and excitation spectra.

The carbon dots suspension was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 min and subsequently dried on ultrathin carbon support films. The morphology of the carbon dots was analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra were measured using a UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer (UH4150, Hitachi, Japan). 0.4 g of dry DA-CGs were placed in a powder cell kit with a diameter of 2 cm for measurement. Wet DA-CGs were also measured using the same method, with 0.4 g of dry DA-CGs pre-wetted by pure water. UV–Vis absorption spectra of water samples containing MO, MB, Cu2+ or CrO4 2− were measured using a UV-3600i Plus (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrometer.
Raman spectra were acquired under an excitation wavelength of 532 nm using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba, Japan). The deconvoluted peaks were fitted using Gaussian functions.
X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were measured using an XRD spectrometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) with a copper target, at a scanning rate of 1°/min.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (EscaLab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to measure the carbon, oxygen and nitrogen content and related functional group constitution of the dried carbon dots. The deconvoluted peaks were fitted using Gaussian functions.
Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired in attenuated total reflectance mode (ATR) using a FT-IR spectrometer (VERTEX 70, Bruker, Germany).

The morphology of DA-CGs and CGs was observed with a field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Regulus 8220, Hitachi, Japan) under the electron beam condition of 3 kV and 10 μA. To observe the morphology of the surface of DA-CGs as they piled in the evaporator, the DA-CGs granules were glued together using a low-viscosity (2–3 cps) liquid adhesive (Guangzhou Jinggu Adhesive Co., Ltd., China) with a slow curing speed. The glued DA-CGs pile was then attached to a 45° or 90° angled SEM sample stub using conductive double-sided tape. All the samples were coated with platinum using an ion sputter coater (MC1000, Hitachi, Japan) under the condition of 10 mA for 120 s before observation.

The evaporation enthalpy of pure or saline water in DA-CGs was isothermally measured at 45 °C for 30 min using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 250, TA Instruments, USA), with the completely wet DA-CGs placed in an aluminum pinhole hermetic pan. The weight of the wet DA-CGs and the aluminum pan was measured together before and after the DSC measurement to calculate the water loss. The evaporation heat flow (in Watts) recorded by DSC was divided by the water loss to determine the normalized evaporation heat flow (in W/g).
The concentrations of Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu(II) and Cr(VI) in the water samples were determined using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (7800(MS), Agilent, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Indoor simulated solar-driven water evaporation tests
ce:section.ce:para: Indoor solar-driven water evaporation tests were performed using a simulated AM1.5G solar spectral irradiance light source (CELS500, China Education AuLight Technology Co., Ltd., China). The light irradiance was measured by a thermal power sensor (S401C, Thorlabs, USA) attached to a digital handheld optical power and energy meter console (PM100D, Thorlabs, USA). The mass change of DA-CGs evaporator, indicative of water loss, was measured every 20 s using an electronic balance (BCE224I, Sartorius, Germany), which was capable of transmitting data to a computer file in real-time. Additionally, the temperature distribution and ramping rate of the DA-CGs pile were monitored and recorded using a thermal infrared imager (E52, FLIR, Inc., USA). Moreover, a white hard sheet made of wood pulp, with a circular hole of 16 mm radius, was placed on the top of the evaporator. This simple wood pulp aperture was conducive to reducing the systematic error caused by excess light irradiation on the foam layer and the beaker. During the most evaporation tests, the room temperature was approximately 25 °C and the relative humidity varied between 50 % and 60 %. The long-term evaporation test for 3.5 wt% sodium chloride was performed under a room temperature of approximately 20 °C and a relative humidity of 20–30 %.

Photothermal temperature ramping tests were conducted using the simulated solar light source and thermal infrared imager. Dried samples were filled into a cubic hole of 2.5 × 2.5 × 2.5 mm on a polypropylene foam beneath the light source. The surface temperatures of the dried samples were monitored using the thermal infrared imager.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water evaporation tests under dark condition
ce:section.ce:para: Water evaporation tests under dark condition were performed in a climatic chamber (LHS-80HC-II, Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China) with controlled temperature and humidity. DA-CGs evaporator and a flat-mouth round test tube with a diameter of approximately 15 mm, were placed in the chamber, both filled with pure water or saline water. The set temperature and relative humidity were 45 °C and 45 %, respectively. However, possibly due to potential redistribution effects of temperature and humidity, the temperature and relative humidity near the DA-CGs evaporator and the test tube, measured using a temperature and humidity sensor (JHT, JAALEE Technology CO., Ltd., China), were approximately 42 °C and 50–60 %, respectively. The weights of the DA-CGs evaporator and the test tube were measured before and after the test conducted over 12 h. Dark evaporation rates were then calculated accordingly.

Outdoor solar-driven water desalination tests were conducted under natural sunlight irradiance at the Yanqi Lake Campus of University of Chinese Academy of Science, located in the Huairou district of Beijing, China (40°24′N, 116°41′E), from August to September 2024. The design of the desalination device features transposed right trapezoidal prism shape, with a 20 × 20 cm square bottom surface, a height of 20 cm on the higher side and 10 cm on the lower side, forming a 45° slanted top. The device was assembled from custom transparent acrylic plates, each 3 mm thick. The inner surface of the device was coated with a super hydrophilic nano layer (contact angle less than 5°) to prevent mist formation and ensure effective sunlight transmission. For outdoor desalination test, the DA-CGs evaporator was expanded to a square liquid container with an inner bottom surface measuring approximately 9.2 × 9.2 cm and a height of 5 cm. The container was filled with actual sea water, and a 9.2 × 9.2 cm square polypropylene foam layer was floated on the water surface. A piece of filter paper was put on top of the foam layer to facilitate sea water absorption, with DA-CGs uniformly distributed on the filter paper as the photothermal evaporation material. The mass change of the evaporator was recorded at specific time intervals to estimate the water yield. The accumulated solar irradiation (MJ/m2) over a given time span was measured by a portable weather station (YG-BX, Wuhan Chenyun Technology Co., Ltd., China). The accumulated solar irradiation was then divided by the corresponding time span to calculate the average solar irradiance. Air temperature was also recorded by the weather station.

In this study, spent coffee grounds were converted into an efficient photothermal conversion material, referred to as DA-CGs for solar-driven interfacial water evaporation. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , DA-CGs (Fig. 1c) were prepared using spent coffee grounds (Fig. 1a) and dopamine through a one-step hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 1b), in which carbon dots (Fig. 1d) were formed on the granules (Fig. 1e) of DA-CGs. These carbon dots, referred to as C-CDs, exhibit significant photothermal conversion properties. In this process, dopamine was used to dope nitrogen into C-CDs, modulating their surface electron defects and enhancing light absorption. Compared to other studies utilizing spent coffee grounds as solar water evaporation materials [15,16,23,36], the hydrothermal preparation method employed in this study is simple and environmental-friendly, using only a one-step reaction and non-toxic materials. In addition, the mild hydrothermal conditions preserved the native micropores on the granules and the hydrophilic cellulose frameworks, which potentially enhanced water evaporation by lowering the evaporation enthalpy. Furthermore, the granules of DA-CGs were wetted and piled up (Fig. 1f), forming a canyon-like surface (Fig. 1g) for solar water evaporation. This uneven surface, resembling commonly reported 3D solar water evaporators but on a more microscopic scale [3,37], can increase the liquid-vapor interfacial area and enhance sunlight trapping, further facilitating solar water evaporation. Notably, this microstructure is formed by simply laying out the coffee grounds on substrates, without the need of complex technologies such as additive manufacturing, nanoimprint lithography and photolithography.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and TEM were applied to investigate the optical properties and morphology of C-CDs. The PL excitation and emission curves of C-CDs were shown in Fig. 2a, under 340 nm excitation, C-CDs presented a maximum emission peak at 430 nm. TEM image in Fig. 2b further verifies the C-CDs have an average diameter of 2.5 nm. In high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b, inset), C-CDs display characteristic lattice fringes with a spacing of 2.48 Å, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene.

XRD and Raman spectroscopy were employed to further investigate the carbon structure of C-CDs and DA-CGs. The XRD patterns of both materials are presented in Fig. 2c. The XRD spectrum of DA-CGs displayed a sharp peak at 21.4°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of disordered graphite (or turbostratic carbon), as compared to the (002) peak of ordered graphite at 26.3° [38,39]. In contrast, the XRD spectrum of C-CDs exhibited a broadened peak around 24.5°, which is closer to the (002) peak of ordered graphite at 26.3°. Along with the lattice fringes observed in high-resolution TEM, this suggests the presence of partially aligned stacking of graphene layers within C-CDs. The broadening of the C-CDs peak can be ascribed to the grain size broadening effect or surface defects of the carbon dots [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the Raman spectra of both C-CDs and DA-CGs exhibited the characteristic D band around 1350 cm−1 and G band around 1580 cm−1. The D band indicates the presence of defects or disorder in the carbon structure while the G band corresponds to graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon networks. The ID/IG values of C-CDs and DA-CGs are 2.86 and 3.17, respectively, indicating high degree of surface defects or structural disorder in both samples. The slightly lower ID/IG value of C-CDs compared to DA-CGs suggests that C-CDs exhibit a higher degree of graphitization, due to their graphitic core structure rich in sp2-hybridized carbon, whereas the bulk DA-CGs likely contain additional components such as amorphous carbon, which contribute to the increased disorder.

The chemical composition of C-CDs and DA-CGs was investigated using FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectra of CGs, DA-CGs, C-CDs and dopamine hydrochloride (DA) were shown in Fig. 2e. The peak near 3308 cm−1 is associated with -OH or NH2 stretching. In the spectrum of CGs, the peaks located at 1652 cm−1, 1461 cm−1 and around 1027 cm−1 correspond to aromatic CC bonds, -CH2- bending and C-O-C bonds, respectively [42,43]. In the spectrum of DA-CGs, both the CC bonds and the -CH2- bonds exhibit a red shift, indicating changes in the chemical structure following the hydrothermal treatment of DA-CGs. In addition, the broad peak of CO bonds slightly diminished in the spectrum of DA-CGs compared to CGs, likely due to the breakage of glycosidic bonds in polysaccharides and the degradation of hemicellulose in CGs. In the spectrum of C-CDs, the strong band for CO vibrations observed at 1563 cm−1 is associated to carbonyl groups in the carbonaceous cores of C-CDs [44]. The peaks located at around 1670 cm−1 and 1200–1300 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of -NH3 group and CN stretching, respectively [44,45]. These nitrogen-related peaks are also weakly present in in the spectrum of dopamine hydrochloride, confirming slight nitrogen doping in C-CDs. XPS survey (Fig. 2f) of the element content confirms the successful nitrogen doping in the C-CDs, consistent with the FT-IR spectra. Specifically, element composition of C-CDs was determined to be 71.71 % carbon, 25.76 % oxygen and 2.53 % nitrogen. The XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) was deconvoluted into four distinct peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.2 eV, corresponding to carbon in CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively. The XPS N1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) was divided into two distinct peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.4 eV, attributed to amino nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen [46]. Similarly, the XPS O1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) was deconvoluted into two distinct peaks at 531.7 eV and 533.3 eV, corresponding to oxygen in CO and CO bonds, respectively [45]. These deconvoluted high resolution XPS results indicate the predominant presence of sp2-hybridized carbon (C=C) and carbon in carbonyl groups (C=O), both of which are responsible for forming the carbonaceous cores of the carbon dots [46]. Additionally, a small amount of graphitic nitrogen appears to be doped into the graphitic carbonaceous core, which may contribute to the extraordinary photothermal conversion effects of C-CDs [34]. The three high resolution XPS spectra, in conjunction with the FT-IR analysis, suggest that C-CDs are functionalized with hydroxyl groups (-OH), amino groups (−NH3) and carbonyl groups (C=O) as the primary surface groups. These surface groups may contribute to the relatively high ID/IG values and increased defect ratio, as confirmed by the Raman spectra, which enhance light absorption and alter the nonradiative relaxation process [47,48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and solar-driven water evaporation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal conversion properties of DA-CGs were first investigated by measuring their reflectance of UV–Vis-NIR diffuse light in both dry and wet states. As shown in Fig. 3a, the diffuse light reflectance of DA-CGs in wet state is lower than that in dry state. This decrease is attributed to the light absorption capacity of water and changes in the light path caused by refraction and internal reflectance within the water, thereby trapping light more effectively [14]. The reflectance was compared to the normalized reference AM1.5 global spectral irradiance, indicating that DA-CGs exhibit an excellent capacity for sunlight trapping [49]. The average absorptances in the solar spectrum ranged from 300 to 2500 nm were calculated to be 88.1 % for dry DA-CGs and 94.6 % for wet DA-CGs. To further assess the photothermal conversion capability of DA-CGs, the surface temperatures of dried samples under one sun irradiance were measured by thermal infrared imager. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, compared to CGs, DA-CGs and C-CDs exhibited a significantly higher initial temperature ramping rate. The surface temperatures of DA-CGs, C-CDs and CGs reached 72.1 ± 1.5 °C, 68.3 ± 0.1 °C and 55.4 ± 2.1 °C within the first 60 s, respectively. As the temperature of the sample increases, the energy accumulation, energy input and energy output of the sample gradually reaches equilibrium, leading to the stabilization of temperature at a steady value. After approximately 200 s, the surface temperature of DA-CGs, C-CDs and CGs stabilized at 76.5 ± 1.8 °C, 73.3 ± 0.4 °C and 62.5 ± 1.6 °C, respectively. These results of photothermal temperature ramping tests indicate that both DA-CGs and the C-CDs extracted from DA-CGs exhibited superior capability of photothermal conversion compared to pristine spent coffee grounds.

The solar-driven water evaporation performance of the DA-CGs evaporators was evaluated. The DA-CGs evaporators and experimental setup for the indoor simulated solar water evaporation tests were illustrated in Fig. 3d, detailed in Experimental Section. The evaporator basically consisted of three main components: a beaker serving as a water container, two circular white polypropylene foam pieces acting as isothermal layers, and a piece of filter paper placed between the two foam layers for water absorption. The beaker was filled with either pure water or sodium chloride solutions. Given that the average salinities of normal seawater and the Dead Sea are 3.5 wt% and 10 wt%, respectively, sodium chloride solutions at these concentrations were used to simulate the two types of seawater in the indoor solar evaporation tests [50]. The bottom foam layer floated freely on the liquid surface and was fully covered by the filter paper. The top foam layer upon the filter paper featured a circular through-hole. Finally, the DA-CGs, pre-wetted with pure water or sodium chloride solutions accordingly, were piled into the through-hole on the top foam layer and manually leveled to align with the height of the top foam layer using a lab spatula, completing the assembly of the DA-CGs evaporator. During the water evaporation tests under 1 sun irradiance, it can be observed in Fig. 3e and f that the surface temperature of the DA-CGs pile increased rapidly within the first 15 min and gradually reached a steady state after 30 min. The average equilibrium surface temperatures of the DA-CGs pile for pure water, 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution and 10 wt% sodium chloride solution were measured to be around 46.9 °C, 46.0 °C and 49.8 °C, respectively. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 3g and h, the water evaporation rates, calculated from mass change data of the DA-CGs evaporator for pure water, 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution or 10 wt% sodium chloride solution, were 2.45 kg m−2 h−1, 2.44 kg m−2 h−1 and 2.35 kg m−2 h−1, respectively. These results indicate the high-performance photothermal water evaporation capability of the DA-CGs evaporator, which exceeds that of previously reported materials derived from coffee grounds, as listed in Table S1. In addition, the evaporation rates under 0.5, 2 and 3 sun irradiances were also evaluated to be 1.05 kg m−2 h−1, 3.60 kg m−2 h−1 and 6.39 kg m−2 h−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1. These results confirm the stability and high performance of the DA-CGs evaporator across varying solar irradiance, showing its potential for real-world desalination applications under diverse climatic conditions or solar still configurations.

The long-term stability of the DA-CGs evaporators in solar water evaporation was assessed through a 96-h indoor test under 1 sun irradiance, using the 3.5 wt% or 10 wt% sodium chloride solution replenished every 12 h. Details on the long-term evaporation tests are provided in Note S1. Over time, edge-preferred salt precipitation was observed on the foam ring surrounding the wet DA-CGs pile but not on the wet DA-CGs pile itself, forming an 3D igloo-like crust that eventually impacted light exposure and vapor transport. As shown in Fig. 3i, when evaporating the 10 wt% sodium chloride solution, although the evaporation rate remained stable for the first 48 h, it showed a slight decline thereafter, likely due to the crust formed by massive salt accumulation. Removing the crust restored the evaporation rate to its initial level, highlighting the effectiveness of DA-CGs evaporators for extended use in highly saline water. The issue of the edge-preferred salt precipitation, where a 3D circular crust partially obstructs the incident light beam and vapor transport, may stem from our specific experimental setup, which is primarily intended for measuring evaporation rates. This problem can potentially be addressed through directional salt precipitation systems, such as gravity-assisted self-cleaning designs [51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of the hydrothermal treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, dopamine was utilized as a nitrogen-containing reagent to modulate the surface electron defects and light absorption properties of C-CDs. Section 3.1 has confirmed the successful nitrogen doping in C-CDs. However, dopamine itself can generate carbon dots through hydrothermal treatment [53]. The formation of C-CDs could potentially result from dopamine alone or from the combined contribution of dopamine and spent coffee grounds. To further investigate the role of dopamine in the generation of C-CDs, spent coffee grounds and dopamine were individually subjected to the hydrothermal treatment, resulting in HT-CGs and HT-DA, respectively. The carbon dots suspensions from HT-CGs and HT-DA were obtained using the same method as for C-CDs and are referred to as C0-CDs and D-CDs, respectively. PL excitation and emission curves of C0-CDs and D-CDs were measured, as shown in Fig. S5. C0-CDs exhibited a maximum PL emission peak at 440 nm under 360 nm excitation. The PL emission peak of C-CDs shifted relative to that of C0-CDs, indicating that the involvement of dopamine altered the PL properties of the coffee-derived carbon dots. In addition, D-CDs exhibited a maximum PL emission peak at 310 nm under 280 nm excitation, which differs significantly from that of C-CDs. These results confirm that C-CDs are formed through the combined contribution of dopamine and spent coffee grounds.

Spent coffee grounds, being typical lignocellulosic biomass, primarily consist of lignin, hemicellulose, and cellulose. To further investigate the roles of these components in the formation of C-CDs, their model components were individually subjected to the hydrothermal treatment with dopamine. Full details on the roles of lignocellulosic components are provided in Note S2. PL analysis of carbon dots suspensions from these hydrothermally treated model components indicated that C-CDs primarily derived from lignin, while hemicellulose and cellulose do not participate in the carbon dots formation (Fig. S6). Furthermore, ATR-FTIR analysis of these hydrothermally treated model components revealed that hemicellulose degrades significantly under hydrothermal conditions, while cellulose remain relatively stable (Fig. S7).

Finally, to further examine the localization of C-CDs within DA-CGs, washing experiments were conducted (Note S3). The PL analysis of the carbon dots suspension from extensively washed and crushed DA-CGs demonstrated that C-CDs on the DA-CGs granules cannot be simply removed by normal filtration, and is supposed to be located both outside and inside the DA-CGs granules (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the highly efficient photothermal water evaporation
ce:section.ce:para: The DA-CGs evaporators exhibited high-performance evaporation of pure water or saline water under 1 sun irradiance. To further analyze the mechanism of such high performances, the water evaporation enthalpies of pure water or saline water in DA-CGs were isothermally measured under 45 °C using DSC. The normalized heat flow and water evaporation enthalpy were shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. 4a. The evaporation enthalpies of pure water, 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution, and 10 wt% sodium chloride solution were measured to be 2182, 1633, and 1565 J/g, respectively. In contrast, literature values for these enthalpies are reported as 2389, 2322, and 2141 J/g, respectively [54]. The significantly lower enthalpy values observed for water in DA-CGs suggest that DA-CGs effectively reduce the water evaporation enthalpy. This phenomenon of reduced water evaporation enthalpy has been reported in many other studies related to materials for solar water evaporation. According to the studies of Yu's team, the reduction of water evaporation enthalpy can be explained by the existence of intermediate water [55,56]. To explore the mechanism of the enthalpy reduction in this study, we measured the intermediate water content of wet DA-CGs and compared it with that of pure or saline water using Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S10, the OH stretching region of the Raman spectrum can be deconvoluted into five characteristic peaks, which are located around 3014, 3220, 3430, 3572 and 3636 cm−1. It is important to note that these peaks are not limited to pure water but can also be observed in sodium chloride solution [57]. Among them, the two peaks located at 3220 and 3430 cm−1 are ascribed to free water molecules with two protons and two electron pairs. In contrast, the two peaks located at 3572 and 3636 cm−1 are associated with intermediate water molecules, where hydrogen bonds are either partially or fully broken. The weak peak around 3014 cm−1 is believed to arise from Fermi resonance between the overtone of the bending mode [55]. The ratios of intermediate water to free water (IW/FW) are presented in Fig. 4b. The IW/FW ratio in wet DA-CGs is significantly higher than the corresponding pure water or saline water, confirming the ability of DA-CGs to promote the formation of intermediate water, which is possibly due to the presence of hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl groups and amino groups within DA-CGs. Notably, the presence of hydroxyl groups is not confined to C-CDs but is supposedly abundant in the unreacted cellulose remaining in DA-CGs.

Apart from evaporation rate, evaporation energy efficiency is also a significant indicator for evaluating the effectiveness of an evaporator in utilizing the solar energy. In our study, the energy efficiencies of DA-CGs evaporator for pure water, 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution and 10 wt% sodium chloride solution under 1 sun irradiation were calculated to be 148.5 %, 110.7 %, 102.2 %. Although the evaporation enthalpies of water in DA-CGs were isothermally measured by DSC, the calculated values still exceed the limitation of thermodynamics. However, the evaporation energy efficiency derived from water evaporation enthalpy represents apparent energy efficiency, contributed by both the solar energy input and environmental energy input. According to Gu's team, under relatively low humidity and solar energy input, apparent energy efficiency can possibly exceed 100 % [58]. In other words, when relative humidity of the environment does not reach saturation point, even without solar energy input, evaporators with well-designed 3D structures can effectively absorb energy from the environment to facilitate water evaporation, due to enlarged liquid-vapor interfacial area and optimization of air moisture transport. This type of evaporation is also commonly referred to as dark evaporation and has been widely studied [1,3,58]. We also measured the dark evaporation rates of the DA-CGs evaporator for both pure and saline water using a climatic chamber. For comparison, the DA-CGs evaporator filled with pure or saline water was tested alongside a corresponding water sample with the same apparent evaporation area under identical conditions. As shown in Fig. 4c, the dark evaporation rate of pure or saline water in the DA-CGs evaporator is significantly higher than the corresponding pure or saline water alone. This result indicates that the DA-CGs evaporator has the potential to utilize environmental energy and promote the water evaporation under dark condition. This potential is attributed to the hierarchical 3D structures of DA-CGs, as observed using SEM. Fig. 4d and e shows the granule surface of CGs and DA-CGs at the micro-nano scale, respectively. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the granule surface of DA-CGs became significantly rougher, with numerous nanospheres ranging in size from tens to hundreds of nanometers forming on it. These nanospheres, also visible on granule surface of hydrothermal treated coffee grounds without adding dopamine (Fig. S11), are ascribed to carbon spheres derived from lignin or polysaccharides through hydrothermal carbonization [12]. Due to their high surface-area-to-volume ratio, these nanospheres can increase the granule surface area at nanoscale, potentially enhancing sunlight absorption and expanding the liquid-vapor interface for improved evaporation efficiency. In addition, at the microscale, multiple pores with tens of micrometers can be observed on the pristine CGs granules (Fig. 4f). These micropores, featuring nodular structures, are possibly the major parenchymatous cell walls of ground coffee beans [59]. Comparable micropores with thinner walls were also observed on the DA-CGs granules (Fig. 4g), suggesting that the hydrothermal treatment did not significantly alter the native porous structure. When the wet extent of DA-CGs is optimal, this porous structure can promote the formation of capillary water, thereby facilitating water evaporation due to the enlarged liquid-vapor interface [1]. Moreover, the less microscopic surface morphology of the granular DA-CGs pile is examined using low magnification mode. Fig. 4h, captured by placing a fixated DA-CGs pile on a 45° angled sample stub, intuitively shows the canyon-like micro-3D surface of the DA-CGs pile. To better observe surface fluctuations, the cross section and surface outline of the DA-CGs pile were also examined (Fig. 4i). The maximum peak-to-valley height of surface outline is measured to be 1.14 mm, representing the surface roughness of DA-CGs pile. The SEM results above indicate that the DA-CGs granules possess numerous nanospheres formed through hydrothermal treatment as well as native micropores formed by cell walls, while DA-CGs pile has canyon-like micro-3D surface due to the random piling of the granules in various irregular shapes. These rough surface structures at both nanoscale and microscale achieve the hierarchical architecture of DA-CGs, further enhancing the evaporation performance by promoting the formation of capillary water or expanding the liquid-vapor interface.

Moreover, we proposed that both the canyon-like micro-3D surface and the native micropores formed by cell walls also play a significant role in sunlight trapping. In Section 3.2, the UV–Vis-NIR diffuse light reflectance of DA-CGs was measured using uncompressed DA-CGs granules, which demonstrated excellent sunlight trapping capacity. To evaluate the contribution of 3D canyon-like surface to this capacity, DA-CGs granules were compressed into a flat film for measurement of UV–Vis-NIR diffuse light reflectance. SEM images taken at both 45° and 90° (Fig. S12a and S12b) reveal the less coarse surface of the compressed film compared to the uncompressed DA-CGs pile. The native micropores of coffee grounds cannot be observed in the compressed DA-CGs film, indicating that this native porous structure was destroyed during the compression. The maximum peak-to-valley height of the surface outline of the compressed DA-CGs film is calculated to be 62 μm, approximately 20 times lower than that of the DA-CGs pile. UV–Vis-NIR diffuse light reflectance (Fig. S12c) of the compressed DA-CGs film was higher than that of the DA-CGs granules. The average absorptance of the DA-CGs film in the solar spectrum were calculated to be 83.7 %, as compared to 88.1 % for DA-CGs granules. As illustrated in Fig. S12d, these results indicate that the microstructures of DA-CGs contributes to their excellent sunlight trapping capacity by enhancing internal light scattering and reducing light reflection, therefore further promoting the evaporation performance.

Outdoor solar desalination tests were conducted to assess the water purification performance of DA-CGs evaporator under real environmental conditions using actual seawater as the feedstock. The design of the solar desalination device, depicted in Fig. 5a, features a transposed right trapezoidal prism shape. As shown in Fig. 5b, the outdoor tests were carried out on the roof of a four-story building, with atmospheric data monitored using a portable weather station. Fig. 5c presents the variations of hourly water yield, average solar irradiance and average air temperature from 8:00 to 18:00 on a given day. The total 10-h water yield, cumulative solar irradiation and average temperature for the day are 6.98 kg m−2, 21.59 MJ m−2 and 32.9 °C, respectively. The maximum hourly water yield reached 1.03 kg m−2 h−1, while the average water yield over the 10-h period was 0.70 kg m−2 h−1. From 8:00 to 12:00, the water yield, solar irradiance, and air temperature progressively increased, peaking around noon. Subsequently, from 12:00 to 18:00, the water yield and solar irradiance gradually declined, while the air temperature remained relatively high, likely due to the continued accumulation of solar heat. Fig. S13 indicates that the concentrations of the four primary ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+) in seawater after the desalination test significantly decreased by three to four orders of magnitude, reaching the WHO standard of drinking water [60]. Fig. 5d shows the daily water yields, solar radiation and average air temperatures recorded between 8:00 and 18:00 on seven separate dates. The highest daily water yield, 7.14 kg m−2, was observed on the first date, corresponding to 19.93 MJ m−2 of solar radiation and an average air temperature of 34.0 °C. In contrast, the lowest daily water yield 4.10 kg m−2, was recorded on the fourth date, with 15.26 MJ m−2 of solar radiation and an average air temperature of 27.7 °C. It should be noted that the water yield appeared to be influenced by both solar radiation and air temperature across the different dates. While the solar radiation determines the total energy input for solar desalination, air temperature impacts both the evaporation rate and condensation efficiency. Moreover, seawater in the DA-CGs evaporator was replenished directly at the end of each day, with no salt precipitation observed throughout the 7-day desalination tests. These outdoor desalination results demonstrate highly efficient water purification capabilities of the DA-CGs evaporator in real-world conditions.

In addition to solar desalination, the DA-CGs evaporator was evaluated for wastewater purification, targeting organic dyes, heavy metals, and strong acids/bases. Specifically, the purification performance was tested using six types of contaminant solutions: 100 mg/L MO (methyl orange), 100 mg/L MB (methylene blue), 1 g/L Cu2+ from CuSO₄, 1 g/L CrO₄2− from K₂CrO₄, 0.5 mol/L H₂SO₄, and 0.5 mol/L NaOH. As shown in Fig. S14, the evaporation rates for these six solutions under 1 sun irradiance were comparable to that for pure water, indicating the high solar wastewater evaporation efficiency of DA-CGs evaporator. In addition, the wastewater purification effects for these solutions were assessed. As shown in the photographs (Fig. S15a), all the colored solutions of organic dyes or heavy meatal ions became colorless after purification. UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S15b-e) confirm the absence of characteristic peaks of these contaminants after purification. ICP-MS measurements (Fig. S16) further confirm that the concentrations of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) were reduced from 791.7 mg/L and 435.9 mg/L to 0.98 mg/L and 0.02 mg/L, respectively. As shown in Fig. S17, the pH value of the H₂SO₄ solution was increased from 0.81 to 6.91, and the pH value of NaOH solution was decreased from 13.54 to 7.29, both achieving almost neutral condition. These results demonstrate that DA-CGs can effectively be utilized for solar-driven wastewater purification and water remediation.

This study developed a biomass-based photothermal water evaporation material, DA-CGs, using only one-step hydrothermal treatment of spent coffee grounds and dopamine. The resulting material contains carbon dots (C-CDs) primarily derived from lignin and doped with nitrogen from dopamine, featuring a high defect ratio and multiple surface functional groups, and demonstrating excellent photothermal conversion properties. DA-CGs reduce water evaporation enthalpy by promoting the formation of more intermediate water. Additionally, DA-CGs feature a hierarchical structure that increases the liquid-vapor interfacial area and enhances sunlight trapping, facilitating efficient photothermal water evaporation. Under 1 sun irradiance, DA-CGs achieve an average absorptance of 94.6 % across the AM1.5 global solar spectrum and demonstrate evaporation rates of 2.45 kg m−2 h−1 for pure water, 2.44 kg m−2 h−1 for 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution and 2.35 kg m−2 h−1 for a 10 wt% sodium chloride solution. Under real-world conditions, a desalination device incorporating the DA-CGs evaporator can produce 7.14 kg m−2 of freshwater daily with an accumulative solar radiation of 19.93 MJ m−2. Furthermore, DA-CGs can be utilized for solar-driven wastewater purification, exhibiting comparable evaporation efficiency and excellent purification effect in water containing organic dyes, heavy metal ions, or strong acids/bases. These results highlight superior photothermal evaporation performance of DA-CGs and potential for scalable, sustainable water purification applications.
